title: My Heart Cannot Bear It When I Think of Those Who Have Fallen So Low!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. All nine source pages were checked for the inspection reports, court directions, Kalaignar's 2010 speech, newspaper quotations, auditorium reports, and closing date. A sentence lost at an OCR page join was visually recovered from source page 202: the Chief Justice stayed the relocation of the Anna Centenary Library. Clearly visible OCR character and word-boundary errors were corrected only in the reading copy.
paragraphs: Jayalalithaa is today the leader of a party that has joined to itself the celebrated name of Perarignar Anna, the embodiment of love, knowledge and strength. In the arrogance of thinking that the people of Tamil Nadu have handed her the authority to rule, how many outrages has she staged during these five years? One? Two? There have been so many!

One of them was the calculated disfigurement of the Anna Centenary Library. Fortunately, intervention by the Madras High Court has kept the library alive. Yet there is no concealing the truth that it has been badly damaged. Because the Jayalalithaa Government repeatedly ignored the High Court's orders and continued its neglect despite every intervention, the Court has now delivered a stern final warning, what is called an “ultimatum.”

When the case concerning the Anna Centenary Library came up on 8 January 2016, the details that emerged in the Madras High Court marked another significant milestone along the course of this litigation.

The Court's inspection committee reported on 8 January that basic infrastructure had still not been improved. The High Court said that unless the books, lights and cameras were repaired and sanitary facilities put in order within one month, the School Education Secretary would have to appear in person.

Jayalalithaa, the present leader of a party bearing the celebrated name of Perarignar Anna, embodiment of love, knowledge and strength, has staged countless outrages during these five years through the arrogance born of ruling power. One was the deliberate disfigurement of the Anna Centenary Library. Fortunately, the Madras High Court's intervention has allowed the library to survive, though there is no denying that it has been left badly damaged.

The Anna Centenary Library, one of Asia's largest libraries, was built and opened under the DMK Government. For that reason, the AIADMK Government failed to carry out proper maintenance. Its efforts to convert the building into a children's hospital and its auditorium into a marriage hall were halted only by court condemnation and opposition from Tamil scholars and students.

In these circumstances, Manonmani filed a case in the Madras High Court alleging that the AIADMK Government continued to neglect the library by neither purchasing necessary books nor maintaining its infrastructure. The First Bench comprising Chief Justice S.K. Kaul and Justice Pushpa Sathyanarayana heard the case.

Following an order of the Bench headed by the Chief Justice on 29 October 2015, Court Commissioners filed reports on 29 November 2015 and 2 January 2016 on whether the Court's directions had been fully implemented and the library maintained.
Senior advocate Wilson appeared and argued again when the matter was heard on 8 January 2016. He said:

> “Five hundred cameras in the Anna Centenary Library are defective. More than two thousand books have been damaged. Because the library has not been properly maintained, readers are suffering and cannot read.”

He further argued that because the Court's order had not been implemented in full, the High Court should initiate contempt proceedings on its own motion against Tamil Nadu Chief Secretary Gnanadesikan, School Education Secretary Sabitha and Director of School Education Dr Kannappan.

Wilson argued that drinking-water facilities, toilets, lighting and lifts had not been fully provided; online facilities had not been established; security equipment intended to prevent books from disappearing did not function properly; surveillance cameras did not record; no steps had been taken to rent out facilities including the auditorium; adequate staff had not been appointed; and service rules had not been framed, leaving library employees without job security.

After recording the deficiencies listed in the Commissioners' reports, the note filed for the petitioner, and senior advocate Wilson's submissions, the Chief Justice directed the Government to implement the Court's order fully and maintain the library properly. The Tamil Nadu Government was given a deadline of 26 February.
Reading this order of the Madras High Court brought many events concerning the Anna Library vividly to my mind. This letter is written to remind you of them.
When I inaugurated the Anna Centenary Library on 15 September 2010, I said:

> “The thought that a library should bear Anna's name did not arise only now. Nor is this merely the implementation of an idea stated in the previous Budget. We have always followed the path of establishing libraries and reading rooms in the names of Anna, Thanthai Periyar and revolutionary poet Bharathidasan, carrying the movement's ideas to every street, town and tiny village across Tamil Nadu. > > “Following that path, when we decided to establish a library now, we felt it must bear Anna's name. We announced it, have implemented it today, and have received all your good wishes.”
That was how I explained the basis for naming the library after Anna.

Driven by uncontrollable jealousy that I had built and opened a library under DMK rule in the name of Arignar Anna, Jayalalithaa has continuously tried, with a petty mind, to disrupt and destroy it. When I think how the dream we cherished in Anna's memory would have been shattered to pieces had the High Court not intervened, how can my heart bear it?

Anna said that every home should have a library. Following that principle, I explained that every city and every district headquarters should have one, that this library had been established on that basis, and that it represented the fulfilment of Anna's dream. How could I ever forget it?
When Jayalalithaa announced that the Anna Centenary Library, a temple of knowledge raised as an embodiment of lofty ideals, would be moved, words could not express the anguish that surged within me.

Chief Minister Jayalalithaa had already overturned schemes conceived for Tamil welfare during the DMK Government. She then announced that the Anna Centenary Library, which I had inaugurated, would also be shifted. When journalists asked me about it, I said only: “I leave this to the judgment of self-respecting Tamils and Tamil scholars.”

Self-respecting Tamils and Tamil scholars, indeed every political party in Tamil Nadu without distinction, were outraged when they learnt of this decision marking the limits of Jayalalithaa's authoritarianism. They strongly opposed it. Newspapers condemned her decision in editorials.
On 4 November 2011, *Theekkathir* wrote:

> “No one could have heard without shock the AIADMK Government's announcement that it would convert the Anna Centenary Library at Kotturpuram, Chennai, into a children's hospital. Countless students, researchers, educationists and legal experts visit every day what functions as South Asia's largest library. It contains books from many fields. Students preparing for the Indian civil-services examination use it daily. The AIADMK Government has once again proved that it is unconcerned about students' welfare.”
Under the headline “Let the Library Stay,” the English edition of *The Hindu* wrote on 5 November 2011:
> “The decision of the Tamil Nadu Government to shift the Anna Centenary Library from the spacious, well-equipped modern building in Kotturpuram, Chennai, is difficult to justify on any ground.”

The English daily *The Times of India* wrote that Jayalalithaa would find no support for her move to dismantle a project of her political opponent Kalaignar; that one cannot succeed in politics by acting against public sentiment; and that the Government would do well to remember that its decision could produce disastrous consequences.

As soon as the Government announced that it would shift the Anna Centenary Library, several self-respecting advocates challenged the move in the Madras High Court. The judges immediately took up and heard the case. In Court, the Chief Justice asked Government counsel why the grand library built at Kotturpuram should be moved, what necessity existed for doing so, and whether counsel was speaking like a politician. The Court stayed the relocation.

On 30 March 2015, *Dinamalar* published a detailed report under the headline, “Anna Library Completely Abandoned by Government; Danger of Paralysis Without Books or Maintenance; People's Tax Money Wasted.” It said that although the Court had stopped the attempt to close the library, it was in fact gradually becoming paralysed.
Library staff and officials told the paper:

> “For the past four years, we have sent many petitions about the library's condition to the School Education Department and the Tamil Nadu Government. But for political reasons they have kept the library immobilised. No Government official comes to inspect it. > > “Because there have been no pay rises, promotions or Government attention, twenty-three people have resigned out of fear that the library will cease functioning. Others are looking for different jobs. Since books are not purchased, the number of readers falls day by day. We had hoped this library would attain international status. We now suffer at the thought that it may be reduced to one ordinary library among many.”
*Dinamalar* thus recorded the anguished feelings of the library employees.

*Dinamani* reported that during a wedding reception held in the Anna Centenary Library complex, the wedding party cooked food within the premises itself. Readers complained that this disturbed the library's quiet and polluted the complex.

*The Times of India* published a photograph and reports under the headlines “Anna Library Auditorium Turns into Marriage Hall,” “Sanctity Breached,” and “The Anna Centenary Library Is Being Let Out for Marriage Receptions in Violation of a Government Order.” It also wrote “People Unhappy” and “Chennaiites Not Happy with the Move.”

On its second page, *The Hindu* carried the large headline “Library Auditorium Turns into Wedding Hall: Officials Term Marriage ‘Cultural Programme’ to Allow Such Functions at Anna Centenary Library.” It reported:

> “The auditorium, considered one of the finest in the city, was also the chosen venue when U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton addressed a huge gathering in the city last July. However, conduct of wedding receptions, with caterers taking up space under a shamiana for cooking and serving, have raised questions about maintenance of the auditorium.”
That was how *The Hindu* described the ruination of the Anna Library.

As soon as I read these reports, I called younger brother and advocate Wilson to my house and asked him to bring the matter immediately to the High Court's attention. He placed it before the Chief Justice. The honourable Chief Justice condemned the renting of the Anna Library for a wedding, instructed that it must never happen again, and said that even if an advance had been received for another wedding reception, it must be returned.

Immediately after the AIADMK Government began, Jayalalithaa announced that the Anna Centenary Library would be shifted to the DPI campus in Egmore and a children's hospital opened in the existing building.
In the case concerning that announcement, the First Bench comprising Chief Justice Iqbal and Justice Sivagnanam observed:
> “Why move such a fine library, created so elegantly with modern facilities? If a hospital is to be built, it could be built on Marina Beach! This decision of the Government is motivated.”

The Madras High Court warned the Tamil Nadu Government that withdrawal of any existing facility or activity at the Anna Centenary Library would be viewed “very seriously” (*Times of India*, 17 February 2012).

After the judges issued those directions, the Government had no choice but to allow the Anna Centenary Library to continue at the location where the DMK Government established it. Yet the Jayalalithaa-led AIADMK Government continued to neglect it stubbornly and carelessly, failing to operate it properly enough for readers to make full use of it. That is why the Madras High Court issued its final warning on 8 January 2016.

Driven by uncontrollable jealousy that I built and opened a library in Arignar Anna's name under the DMK Government, Jayalalithaa has continuously tried, with a petty mind, to disrupt and destroy it. When I think how the dream we cherished in Anna's memory would have been shattered to pieces had the High Court not intervened, how can my heart bear it?
With affection,
M.K.
12 January 2016
